title: Why we don't do fast fashion (and what that costs us)
excerpt: A list of the things we said no to in 2025. None of them made the brand. All of them defined it.
author: The studio
body: We turned down a re-cut order from a Lahore stockist last March. 600 pieces, full margin, two-month turn. We said no.
The pieces in question were from Drop 002 — Monsoon. We'd told everyone who bought it that we wouldn't restock. We meant it. Re-cutting for one retailer breaks the rule for everyone.
That's PKR 1.8M we left on the table. It's also why Drop 003 sold through to people who've been here since 001 instead of people who showed up because Outfitters had the same hoodie.

We also said no to: a polyester base for the Bearly Awake hoodie (saves PKR 600/unit, looks fine, doesn't breathe in Karachi). A licensed Marvel collab (good money, wrong room). A sample sale for influencers (we'd rather pay them after the drop, not bribe them before).
Fast fashion isn't a speed problem. It's a values problem. The speed comes when the values are loose enough to bend.
Drop 004 is two months out. It'll have eight pieces. We'll sell 1,400 of them. The math works because we don't have to feed the machine.

title: A sound system that doesn't exist (yet)
excerpt: Notes on the Karachi music scene we want to build a soundtrack to.
author: Ahad
body: There's a club in DHA that opens at 11pm and closes when the bouncers say so. No name. No flyer. The address is a WhatsApp message you don't get unless someone vouches for you.
We've been three times. Each time the music was different. Trap one week, qawwali edits the next, a guy mixing Coke Studio stems into 145bpm drum & bass after that.
There's no scene here, exactly. There are pockets. Lyari has its own. NUST has another. The afterparties for the comedy nights at PACC are a third. None of them talk to each other yet.
Drop 003 was soundtracked in our heads by the qawwali-drum-and-bass set. Apex utility, Karachi after midnight, sweating through cotton you cut six weeks ago. That's the brief.

We want to put on the night that brings the pockets together. Once a year. Free entry, members get the WhatsApp first. We're not promoters and we won't pretend to be — but the brand needs a soundtrack and Spotify isn't it.
If you DJ and you're in this orbit, DM us. We have one slot left and we're not picky about genre, only about taste.

title: Zaynab, on wearing the same hoodie for 90 days
excerpt: A founding member tells us what happens when one piece becomes a uniform.
author: Hira, with Zaynab
body: Zaynab bought the Bearly Awake hoodie from Drop 002 on a Tuesday in February. She wore it the next day. And the day after. And the day after.
Ninety days, she counted. With breaks for washing — she'd wear it Monday-Tuesday-Wednesday, wash Wednesday night, back on Thursday-Friday. Weekends she said she let it rest.

We asked why. She said she'd never had a piece she didn't want to take off. She said the cotton softened the way old denim does — uneven, in the places her body touched it first. She said people stopped commenting on it after week three.
We asked what changed. She said she stopped thinking about what to wear. She said she could spend that brain on better questions.
We're not telling you to wear one thing for 90 days. We're saying: if you've never had a piece you wanted to, that's the gap we're trying to make pieces for.
